papers.title: Runaway Stars, Hypervelocity Stars, and Radial Velocity Surveys
papers.author_names: Benjamin C. Bromley Scott J. Kenyon Warren R. Brown Margaret J. Geller

abstract.text: Runaway stars ejected from the Galactic disk populate the halo of the Milky Way. To predict the spatial and kinematic properties of runaways, we inject stars into a Galactic potential, compute their trajectories through the Galaxy, and derive simulated catalogs for comparison with observations. Runaways have a flattened spatial distribution, with higher velocity stars at Galactic latitudes less than 30 degrees. Due to their shorter stellar lifetimes, massive runaway stars are more concentrated towards the disk than low mass runaways. Bound (unbound) runaways that reach the halo probably originate from distances of 6--12 kpc (10--15 kpc) from the Galactic center, close to the estimated origin of the unbound runaway star HD 271791. Because runaways are brighter and have smaller velocities than hypervelocity stars (HVSs), radial velocity surveys are unlikely to confuse runaway stars with HVSs. We estimate that at most 1 runaway star contaminates the current sample. We place an upper limit of 2% on the fraction of A-type main sequence stars ejected as runaways.

triage.reason: Direct title match: hypervelocity stars, runaway stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper uses Galactic potential modeling to simulate the trajectories of runaway stars ejected from the Galactic disk, predicting their spatial and velocity distributions as a function of mass. It finds that runaways exhibit a flattened spatial distribution with higher velocities at Galactic latitudes below 30°, and that massive runaways are more disk-concentrated than low-mass ones; bound runaways originate from 6–12 kpc, unbound from 10–15 kpc, consistent with HD 271791's origin. Runaway stars are distinguishable from hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by lower velocities and brightness, with at most 1 runaway contaminating current HVS samples and an upper limit of 2% on A-type main sequence stars ejected as runaways.

source_fields.title: Runaway Stars, Hypervelocity Stars, and Radial Velocity Surveys

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: This section concludes that runaway stars, ejected primarily via binary-supernova or dynamical mechanisms, exhibit distinct kinematic and spatial properties linking the Galactic disk to the halo, with simulations showing they are concentrated near the Galactic plane, have velocities and distributions intermediate between the thick disk and halo, and are unlikely to be confused with high-velocity stars in radial velocity surveys—highlighting the need for better observations and simulations to constrain their formation rates and origins. Introduction: This paper presents a novel simulation of runaway star formation and evolution, predicting their spatial and velocity distributions in the Galactic halo as a function of mass to address the lack of observational constraints on runaway star populations and to assess their potential contamination of hypervelocity star searches. The Simulations: This section describes a 3D numerical simulation framework that models the ejection, propagation, and final spatial and velocity distributions of runaway stars in the Milky Way, using realistic initial conditions and a galactic potential derived from observational data. Application to Hypervelocity Stars: This section demonstrates that runaway stars and hypervelocity stars (HVSs) exhibit distinct spatial, luminosity, and velocity distributions—runaways are more concentrated in the Galactic disk, brighter, and show rotational velocity patterns due to disk ejection, while HVSs are fainter, faster, and radially ejected, with simulations showing that runaways contribute minimally to the observed HVS sample. Application to the Hyper-Runaway HD 271791: The runaway origin of HD 271791 is more plausibly explained by dynamical ejection in a dense stellar cluster than by a supernova explosion in a massive binary, due to the high observed velocity exceeding the maximum achievable in binary models. Application to Halo Radial Velocity Surveys: This section demonstrates that runaway stars are more abundant at low Galactic latitudes than in the halo population and sets an upper limit on the fraction of A-type runaways using combined photometric and spectroscopic survey data, highlighting their distinct kinematic and metalliclicity properties.

abstract: Runaway stars ejected from the Galactic disk populate the halo of the Milky Way.
Runaways have a flattened spatial distribution, with higher velocity stars at Galactic latitudes less than 30 degrees.
Due to their shorter stellar lifetimes, massive runaway stars are more concentrated towards the disk than low mass runaways.
Because runaways are brighter and have smaller velocities than hypervelocity stars (HVSs), radial velocity surveys are unlikely to confuse runaway stars with HVSs.
We place an upper limit of 2% on the fraction of A-type main sequence stars ejected as runaways.
title: Runaway Stars, Hypervelocity Stars, and Radial Velocity Surveys

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical simulation paper that creates simulated catalogs for comparison with observations, but does not present actual observational stellar data. Mentions HD 271791 only as a consistency check for their model.
